response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion:
I've been using social media for years now, mostly scrolling through news feeds during my breaks at work as an electrician. It's interesting how the internet has changed everything, but sometimes I feel overwhelmed by all this fake stuff floating around. You know, like when I first saw that article claiming birds can talk to cats or something ridiculous—I had to check it twice because I'm a curious guy who likes birdwatching himself. My dad used to be an engineer with a PhD and he always said there's truth in numbers, so I've gotten into looking at statistics on these posts too.

As someone from New York City, I see all kinds of content every day—people posting about their electrician services or the latest political nonsense. Being divorced and not having any kids means I spend a lot of time alone during work hours, but that's also why I enjoy diving into social media to stay connected with my Muslim friends back home.

I think managing fake content requires some patience and knowing when to call BS on it. My lower scores in conscientiousness mean I don't overanalyze things too much—just take a quick look if something seems off—but agreeableness keeps me from being harsh about it right away.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary:
Based on my background as a curious, part-time electrician with some exposure to technical thinking (from my dad's PhD), I approach fake content by first questioning its validity through simple checks. Living in New York and having spent several hours daily online makes me sensitive to misinformation circulating there.

My low extraversion means social interactions don't energize me as much, but since I'm already spending time on these platforms anyway, it's more about managing frustration than seeking engagement with others who might believe the fake stuff. As a Muslim in New York, I do pay attention to content related to my faith or city life.

Given my lower conscientiousness scores (8 out of 100), I rely heavily on heuristic methods like checking sources quickly rather than deep dives into verification processes.
response_parsed.tone: Observational with a hint of skepticism
citations: Various online articles and personal observations
